items.context: Problem 7, definition of the variational form whose constrained minimum is to be attained.
items.message: The displayed variational form places the lower-order terms inside the scope of the outer sum over \(i,j\), while also using an inner sum over \(i\). This potentially unintended summation scope has been retained because the intended correction is not uniquely determined by the immediate context.
items.original_text: \(I(w)=\int_{B_1}\sum_{i,j=1}^n\left(a_{ij}\partial_iw\partial_jw+\sum_i b_i\partial_iw\,w+cw^2\right)dx\)

items.context: Problem 6 and its three-part hint on source page 2.
items.message: Problem 6 uses \(q\) without defining it. Also, the retained Hint 2, \(g=|f|^{p-1}\in L^q\), does not account for the sign of a general \(f\), and the endpoint \(p=1\) requires separate treatment. The intended correction is not uniquely determined from the immediate context.
items.original_text: Fix 1 ≤ p < ∞, and let f ∈ L^p(X, A, μ). Show that ‖f‖_p = sup{∫fg dμ : ‖g‖_q ≤ 1}. Hint: 2. Show that g = |f|^{p−1} ∈ L^q.

items.context: Problem 8, Part (b): “Define two new measures μ and ν ... by setting μ(A) = ∫_A x dx whenever A ∈ C.”
items.message: The source visibly defines \(\mu(A)=\int_A x\,dx\) while calling \(\mu\) a measure on the sigma-algebra of symmetric Borel sets. For sets such as \(\mathbb R\), this signed Lebesgue integral is not defined, so the intended integrand may contain an unprinted or unclear modification. No correction is uniquely determined, and the source reading has been retained.

items.context: Prove that the group G of upper triangular 2 by 2 matrices with integer coefficients is a free nilpotent class 2 group on two generators.
items.message: The source clearly appears to say “upper triangular 2 by 2 matrices with integer coefficients,” but as written these matrices do not specify the standard free nilpotent class 2 group on two generators. A possible intended formulation involving upper unitriangular 3 by 3 integer matrices is not uniquely determined from the source, so the source reading has been retained.

items.context: The presentation of the hint group \(G_n\) in Problem 4.
items.message: The displayed indexing condition has been retained as printed, but its scope is mathematically ambiguous because the relation \(yx_iy^{-1}=x_{i+1}\) does not involve \(j\).
items.original_text: \(x_ix_j=x_jx_i,\;yx_iy^{-1}=x_{i+1}\text{ for }1\leq i<j\leq n\)

items.context: Problem 9 defines \(K\) as the splitting field of \(X^p-1\) and then asks for a unique quadratic extension of \(\mathbb Q\) inside \(K\).
items.message: The source says only that \(p\) is prime, but the assertion that \(K\) contains a quadratic extension of \(\mathbb Q\) fails when \(p=2\). A possible omitted restriction cannot be determined uniquely, so the source's mathematical scope has been retained.

items.context: Problem 10 assumes only that \(R\) is a finite-dimensional \(k\)-algebra and is semisimple as a ring.
items.message: Even after the grammatical correction, the printed assertion appears to require an additional hypothesis: a finite-dimensional semisimple \(k\)-algebra need not be a direct sum of fields. No missing hypothesis is uniquely determined, so none has been added.

items.context: The stem does not require the points \(x_n\) to be distinct, while part (b) states \(f(x_n+)-f(x_n-)=c_n\) for each \(n\).
items.message: Part (b) is not valid if the sequence \(\{x_n\}\) contains repeated values: the jump at \(x_n\) would include every \(c_k\) for which \(x_k=x_n\). The intended repair is not unique; the source reading has therefore been retained.

items.context: Prove that if μ(Ω) < ∞ then it follows that f is integrable and ∫Ω f_n dμ → ∫Ω f dμ.
items.message: The source clearly says “Lebesgue-measurable functions,” but measurability and uniform convergence alone do not ensure the stated integrability conclusion, even when the measure is finite. A missing integrability hypothesis may have been intended, but the correction is not uniquely determined, so the source wording has been retained.
items.original_text: a sequence of Lebesgue-measurable functions converging uniformly to f

items.context: The printed definition is \(g(0)=f'(0)\) and \(g(x)=f(x)/x\) for \(0<x<a\), followed by the claim that \(g\) is continuous and strictly increasing on \([0,a)\).
items.message: The problem is preserved as printed, but its conclusion generally fails without an additional condition such as \(f(0)=0\), or without replacing \(f(x)/x\) by \((f(x)-f(0))/x\). The intended repair is not uniquely determined.

items.context: “Given \(f\in L^1(\mu)\), show, by considering the mapping \(\rho:\mathcal N\to[0,\infty)\) defined by \(\rho(E)=\int_E f\,d\mu\)”
items.message: The source assigns \(\rho\) the codomain \([0,\infty)\), although for an arbitrary \(f\in L^1(\mu)\), the values \(\int_E f\,d\mu\) need not be nonnegative. The intended repair is not uniquely determined: the codomain may be misstated, or a nonnegativity assumption on \(f\) may be missing. The source reading has been retained.

items.context: \(\sum_{i=0}^{m}\binom{s}{i}\binom{t}{k-i}=\binom{s+t}{k}\)
items.message: The summation upper limit is clearly printed as \(m\), but \(m\) is otherwise undefined and no conditions are given under which the displayed identity holds. The source reading has been preserved because the intended correction is not uniquely determined.

items.context: Problem 10 asks for an explicit formula for the sequence.
items.message: The recurrence is stated only for \(n\ge 1\), while the sole initial condition is \(a_0=0\); these conditions do not determine \(a_1\) and hence do not uniquely determine the sequence. The intended lower bound or initial condition is unclear, so the source reading has been retained.

items.context: The diagrams occur after “For instance, a(2) = 5, given by” and before “Set a(0) = 1.”
items.message: Five diagrams illustrate the tilings counted by a(2) = 5; the visual examples have not been reconstructed.

items.context: Part (b) asks for a monotone subsequence of length n + 1 in every sequence of length n² + 1 using part (a).
items.message: As printed, part (a) does not yield the Erdős–Szekeres theorem stated in part (b): setting \(r=s=n\) gives only size \(n\), while setting \(r=s=n+1\) does not satisfy the stated threshold. The intended correction is not unique, so the source reading has been retained.
items.original_text: Prove that if P has size at least rs + 1, then there is either a chain of size r or an antichain of size s.

items.context: Both subparts impose parity conditions on the numbers of occurrences of specified letters.
items.message: The problem does not specify the length of the strings, so the requested finite count is underdetermined. A likely missing condition is a fixed length, but the intended wording cannot be determined uniquely from the source.
items.original_text: How many strings can be formed using the alphabet {A, B, C, D, E} if

items.context: The poset is finite and has unique minimal element \(0\).
items.message: The statement as printed is false when the unique element covered by \(y\) is the minimal element \(0\). A condition such as \(x\ne0\) may be missing, but the intended correction is not uniquely determined, so the source reading has been retained.
items.original_text: Let \(y\in P\) so that there is only one \(x\) covered by \(y\). Show \(\mu(0,y)=0\).

items.context: Problem 3 characterizes an entire function of order 1 with zero set \(\{0,\pm1,\pm2,\ldots\}\).
items.message: The source reading is clear, but the hypotheses and conclusion are mathematically inconsistent: \(\sin \pi z\) has derivative \(\pi\), not 1, at 0 and is unbounded on the imaginary axis. The intended correction is not uniquely determined, so the source text has been retained.
items.original_text: Suppose \(f'(0)=1\), \(f(1/2)=1\) and \(f\) is bounded on the imaginary axis. Show that \(f(z)=\sin \pi z\).

items.context: Uniform convergence on compact sets of entire functions.
items.message: The statement is preserved as printed, but it overlooks the possible case \(g\equiv0\), for example when \(g_n\equiv1/n\). The intended correction is not uniquely determined: one could assume \(g\not\equiv0\) or conclude that \(g\) is identically zero or has only real zeros.
items.original_text: If each \(g_n\) has only real zeros, show that \(g\) has only real zeroes.

items.context: Problem 6, the bracketed assertion describing the tangent map of the group operation and defining \(\lambda^a\) and \(\rho^b\).
items.message: The displayed tangent-map formula is type-inconsistent with the definitions that follow it. The maps \(\lambda^a\) and \(\rho^b\) are defined as maps into \(G\times G\), so their tangent vectors lie in \(T_{(a,b)}(G\times G)\), while \(p_*(\xi,\eta)\) lies in \(T_{p(a,b)}G\). More than one repair is possible, so the source reading has been retained.

items.context: Part (1): If w is a unit vector, then H=I−2wwᵗ is an orthogonal projection.
items.message: The source calls H an “orthogonal projection,” but H=I−2wwᵗ is generally a Householder reflection (and an orthogonal matrix), not a projection. The source wording was retained because the intended replacement is not uniquely determined.

items.context: Problem 5(b). With the displayed dimensions, \(A\) is \(m\times n\) and \(B\) is \(n\times q\), so \(BA\) is not generally conformable.
items.message: The displayed dimensions make the product \(BA\) undefined in general. The source reading has been retained because it is unclear whether the dimensions or the product order should be corrected.
items.original_text: If \(A\in\mathbb R^{m\times n}\) and \(B\in\mathbb R^{n\times q}\), then \(\lVert BA\rVert_2\leq\lVert B\rVert_2\cdot\lVert A\rVert_2\).

items.context: Problem 4(a), following the definition of a \(3\times2\) matrix \(A\).
items.message: The source clearly asks for the projection of \(A\) onto its own column space, but the intended object is mathematically ambiguous; it may have intended the orthogonal projection matrix onto the column space. The source wording has been retained.
items.original_text: Determine the orthogonal projection of \(A\) onto the column space of \(A\).

items.context: Problem 4 assumes only that \(A\in\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\) and \(\lVert A\rVert_p<1\); part (a) establishes the invertibility of \(I-A\), not of \(A\).
items.message: Part (c) uses \(A^{-1}\), but the standing assumption \(\lVert A\rVert_p<1\) does not imply that \(A\) is invertible. The source reading has been retained because the intended correction is not uniquely determined.
items.original_text: Prove that \(\lVert A\rVert_q\lVert A^{-1}\rVert_q\geq 1\), \(\forall 1\leq q\leq\infty\).

items.context: Problem 2 in full.
items.message: The source specifies a potentially rectangular matrix as a projector, although projectors and orthogonal projectors ordinarily require a square matrix. In addition, the stated equivalence has the zero orthogonal projector as a possible exception unless nonzero projectors are assumed. The intended dimensions and convention are not uniquely determined, so the source wording has been retained.
items.original_text: Let P ∈ C^{m×n} be a projector. Show that ‖P‖₂ = 1 if and only if P is an orthogonal projector.

items.context: Problem 4(b), following part (a), which assumes rank(A) = p < n < m.
items.message: Part (b) refers to n singular values and asserts σ_n > 0, which requires full column rank and hence m ≥ n. The condition n < m appears only in part (a), so it is unclear whether it is intended to continue into part (b). The source wording has been retained.
items.original_text: Suppose A is full rank and x ≠ 0. Let σ_i, i = 1, ···, n be the singular values of A. Show σ_1 ≥ ‖Ax‖₂/‖x‖₂ ≥ σ_n > 0.

items.context: Problem 2 assumes only that \(A\in\mathbb C^{n\times n}\) and \(\|A\|_p<1\).
items.message: Part (c) is preserved as printed, but the stated assumptions do not ensure that \(A^{-1}\) exists; for example, \(A=0\) satisfies \(\|A\|_p<1\). The intended additional assumption or alteration is not uniquely determined.
items.original_text: Prove that \(\|A\|_q\|A^{-1}\|_q\ge 1\), \(\forall\,1\le q\le\infty\).

items.context: Problem 7 defines a quadratic functional and assumes a coercivity inequality for it.
items.message: Several mathematical symbols are absent from the rendered source: the name of the quadratic functional is missing in its definition and later occurrences, and the positive coercivity constant is missing both after “There exists” and before the integral in the inequality. Descriptive placeholders were retained rather than guessing the symbols.
items.original_text: “Define by (h) := … Suppose that has the following property: There exists > 0 such that (h) ≥ ∫ … Hint: … show that (u) = 0.”

items.context: Problem 7 asks for uniqueness for a wave equation but names an unused function g and gives only the displayed boundary and initial-velocity data.
items.message: The source is internally inconsistent: it states “where g, h are smooth functions,” but g does not occur in the displayed problem, while f does. The stated data may also omit an initial-displacement condition. The intended correction is not uniquely determined, so the source reading has been retained.
items.original_text: u = 0 on Γ_T; u_t = h on U × {t = 0}; where g, h are smooth functions.

items.context: The printed statement concludes that \(\lim b_n=b\in\mathbb R\) solely from convergence of \(f(b_nt)\) to a characteristic function.
items.message: Part (b) is retained as printed, but its conclusion can fail for a nonconstant symmetric characteristic function when the sequence alternates in sign. Any intended additional restriction or alternative conclusion is not uniquely determined.

items.context: Problem 6 asks for the fundamental group of a pictured space. Problem 7 defines the edge identifications of a disk by a picture.
items.message: The figures referenced in problems 6 and 7 carry information needed to answer the questions and have not been reconstructed.

items.context: Problem 3 assumes only that G has order 315; part (i) gives a conclusion conditional on a Sylow 3-subgroup being normal.
items.message: The source clearly states that one should deduce that every group of order 315 is abelian, but this conclusion appears mathematically false; nonabelian groups of order 315 exist. The intended statement is not uniquely determined, so the source reading has been retained.
items.original_text: (ii) Deduce from this that G is abelian.

items.context: “Let \((S_\infty,\Sigma,\mu)\) be a finite measure space,” followed later by \(\int_S\) and “pointwise on \(S\).”
items.message: The underlying space in the opening tuple appears to be printed as \(S_\infty\), but the integral and the later pointwise-convergence statement refer to \(S\). The intended symbol is uncertain, so the apparent source reading has been retained.
items.original_text: \((S_\infty,\Sigma,\mu)\)

items.context: Let μ be the Lebesgue measure on (0,1). Each point x∈(0,1] has a binary expansion.
items.message: The source legibly uses \((0,1)\) for the measure space but \((0,1]\) for the binary-expansion domain; whether this endpoint difference is intentional is unclear.

items.context: Problem 10 uses \(L_F^1(\nu)\) and \(L_F^1(\mu)\), while \(F\) is introduced only in Problem 8.
items.message: The Banach space symbol F used in L_F^1 is not defined within this problem; it may carry over from the first problem in Section III.

items.context: Problem 1 assumes only that \(g\) is continuously differentiable on \([a,b]\).
items.message: Part (v) asks for quadratic convergence under continuous differentiability and \(g'(\alpha)=0\). Those assumptions generally imply superlinear, but not necessarily quadratic, convergence; a stronger regularity assumption may be missing, but the intended correction is not uniquely determined.
items.original_text: Assume in addition that \(g'(\alpha)=0\) and show that the sequence converges quadratically.

items.context: Problem 11 asks whether this family forms a basis for a topology on \(\mathbb{R}\).
items.message: The displayed family of half-open intervals uses \([b,c)\), while its conditions include the otherwise unused variable \(d\). The intended endpoints or inequalities are not uniquely determined, so the source reading has been retained.

items.context: Problem 6 defines a sequence of real-valued functions on all of \(\mathbb R\).
items.message: The displayed definition is mathematically inconsistent over the stated real domain: \(\sqrt{x}\) is not real-valued for negative \(x\). The intended correction to the domain or formula is not uniquely determined, so the source reading has been retained.
items.original_text: \(f_n:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R,\ f_n(x)=\sqrt{x}/n\)

items.context: Describe a space \(X\) with \(\pi(X)\cong\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}\).
items.message: The source clearly displays \(\pi(X)\), though \(\pi_1(X)\) may have been intended; the source notation has been retained.
items.original_text: \(\pi(X)\cong\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}\)

items.context: “Let \(X\) be the space obtained by filling in two discs in the torus, as shown (the discs lie inside the surface of the torus).”
items.message: The problem depends on a figure showing the placement of the two discs in the torus; the visual information has not been reconstructed.

items.context: Consider the torus \(T=S^1\times S^1\) with the following \(\Delta\)-complex structure.
items.message: The diagram supplies essential information about the torus’s Δ-complex structure, including simplex labels and edge orientations, and is not reconstructed in the text extraction.

items.context: “Consider the Δ-complex X drawn below. (Note the orientation of the three edges carefully.)”
items.message: The diagram defining the Δ-complex, including its edge orientations and identifications, carries information needed to solve the problem and is not represented in the extracted text.

items.context: The problem refers to the Δ-complex drawn below.
items.message: The diagram of the Δ-complex contains edge orientations and identifications needed to compute its simplicial homology.

items.context: The problem identifies A as the circle shown in the figure.
items.message: The figure specifies how the circle A is embedded in the solid torus and is necessary for the retraction problem.

items.context: The exam-level assumption states only that \((X,\Sigma,\mu)\) is a finite measure space.
items.message: The source statement was retained, but on a general finite measure space the displayed hypotheses do not imply the stated lower bound. An additional normalization such as \(\mu(X)=1\), or a different lower bound, may have been intended; the correction is not uniquely determined.
items.original_text: Let \(f\geq 0\) be \(\mu\)-integrable such that \(\int f\,d\mu\leq 1\). Prove that \(\int \frac{1}{f}\,d\mu\geq 1\).

purpose: Unresolved cases that require human review before publication.
